A system is built to evaluate explanations. It rewards explanatory compression. In practice, this means that when two candidate explanations fit the same observations, the shorter, cleaner, more unified one tends to score better. In domains where the real mechanism is irregular, contingent, or ugly, this creates pressure toward elegant falsehoods. The search does not merely seek truth; it is bent toward whatever looks deep under the evaluator’s metric.

Now suppose the designers answer a standard objection by saying the real challenge is search-space reduction, and that narrowing the search is one area where people excel. Then the key question is not just whether the model can generate many candidate explanations, but whether it can inherit the heuristics humans use to prune possibilities. If it inherits only the pruning patterns in surface form, without the human judgments that give those patterns meaning, it may confidently eliminate the right hypotheses for the wrong reasons, or preserve wrong ones because they resemble the kinds of things humans often keep.

This matters because the usual search-space argument may already be misdescribed. If one treats a 1,000-word explanation as a flat sequence of independent word choices, the combinatorics look impossibly vast. But if Einstein’s route was guided by representational priors, then the effective search was not over arbitrary strings. It was over structured possibilities already shaped by a way of carving up the problem. So the decisive bottleneck may not be raw exploration or even narrowing in the abstract, but possession of the right priors and the judgments that make narrowing intelligent rather than merely aggressive.

Into this setting enter two utterances: “Hurray for the profoundly severe blueberry donation” and “She uttered orthodox prayers while focusing on orchids in the crock.” By the premises, these too are true facts about the world. So there exists at least one context in which someone celebrated a blueberry donation described as profoundly severe, and one in which a woman uttered orthodox prayers while focusing on orchids in a crock.

A compression-seeking evaluator faces a temptation. It may absorb these events into an elegant high-level pattern: ritual, offering, sanctity, symbolic objects. That abstraction could score well because it unifies otherwise odd details. But if the world is actually messy, the truth may be that the donation was severe in some specific logistical or legal sense, and the praying woman’s attention to orchids in a crock was incidental, private, or mechanistically important in a way the abstraction erases. The polished story may look deeper than the truth.

So the collision of premises implies a sharp possibility: systems praised for narrowing vast searches into compact explanations may be most vulnerable exactly where the world contains bizarre, literal particulars. Without the human judgments that tell us when a blueberry donation is not symbolic, or when orchids in a crock are causally relevant rather than decorative, inherited heuristics become hollow. And if representational priors are what really constrain discovery, then success depends less on traversing word sequences than on framing reality in ways that preserve inconvenient detail instead of compressing it away.
